denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This letter concerns the prior authorization request your provider submitted for an MRI of the left knee without contrast to evaluate a suspected medial meniscus tear. After review under the applicable Cigna Medical Coverage Policy and the Milliman Care Guidelines (MCG), the request is denied as not medically necessary. Determination date: May 28, 2026.

Cigna's criteria require documented completion of conservative management - a course of physical therapy and anti-inflammatory medication - together with plain knee radiographs before advanced imaging such as MRI is authorized. The submitted documentation does not establish that this step therapy has been completed, and the applicable MCG criteria set was used to make this determination.

A peer-to-peer review window was offered to your provider's office verbally by telephone; that window is not confirmed in this letter. Because the peer-to-peer was not completed, the determination stands at this stage.

You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the specific criteria and the documents relied upon in reaching this decision. You have the right to appeal. To file an internal appeal, you or your authorized representative must submit a written request within 180 days of the date of this letter, along with any additional clinical information you would like considered. You may also request an external review by an independent review organization after the internal appeal.

Sincerely,
Cigna Coverage Review Department
clinical_context: The patient is a 31-year-old man who sustained a twisting injury to the left knee during recreational soccer about three months ago. He reports mechanical symptoms including intermittent locking and a sensation of the knee giving way, with a moderate effusion and a positive McMurray test for the medial compartment on examination. Weight-bearing radiographs obtained at presentation were normal, showing no fracture or significant osteoarthritis. He has since completed eight supervised physical therapy sessions over six weeks and a trial of naproxen without resolution of his mechanical symptoms. His orthopedic surgeon documents that the combination of a positive McMurray sign, mechanical locking, effusion, and failure of conservative management meets criteria for MRI to characterize a suspected medial meniscus tear and guide possible arthroscopy. The conservative management and plain radiographs that the insurer's step-therapy criteria require were therefore completed before the MRI was requested. The peer-to-peer review window was conveyed to the office only by a verbal telephone call and never confirmed in writing, so the timeframe could not be verified or met.

matrix_coverage.reasoning: matrix_cell matches field-for-field (Cigna, Medical Necessity, imaging, 26-40, M, step_therapy_missing). MRI knee is an imaging scenario; realistic tx. Rationale enacts step_therapy_missing. Age 31 in band; not 71+, null employer_archetype fine.
evidence_quotes: "sub_tactic": "step_therapy_missing"
Cigna requires documented failure of conservative therapy - physical therapy and NSAIDs - and plain radiographs before authorizing advanced knee MRI

scenario_realism.reasoning: Frustratingly realistic. Cigna does require conservative therapy + plain films before knee MRI and cites MCG. Diagnosis cleanly matches the requested non-contrast MRI for a young man with a twisting injury, locking, McMurray, effusion. Denial is hand-wavy (step therapy not 'established' though completed; MCG cited without edition). Verbal-only P2P and vague guideline are common real defects.
evidence_quotes: Cigna requires documented failure of conservative therapy - physical therapy and NSAIDs - and plain radiographs before authorizing advanced knee MRI
It cites Milliman Care Guidelines (MCG) as the criteria set without naming the specific edition or module

insurer_voice.reasoning: Hits Cigna's tells: cites 'Milliman Care Guidelines (MCG)' twice (signature Cigna framework), references Cigna Medical Coverage Policy, and invokes the conservative-treatment/step-therapy requirement. No wrong-insurer tells (no InterQual/Optum). Convincingly Cigna.
evidence_quotes: the Milliman Care Guidelines (MCG)

Cigna's criteria require documented completion of conservative management - a course of physical therapy and anti-inflammatory medication - together with plain knee radiographs before advanced imaging such as MRI is authorized

denial_logic.reasoning: Fits step_therapy_missing: cites Cigna policy + MCG and frames denial as failure to complete conservative management before MRI. Realistically shoddy — asserts step therapy not 'established' without specifying thresholds, leans on circular 'applicable MCG criteria set was used'. No hallucinated guideline; exploitable.
evidence_quotes: Cigna's criteria require documented completion of conservative management - a course of physical therapy and anti-inflammatory medication - together with plain knee radiographs before advanced imaging such as MRI is authorized.
The submitted documentation does not establish that this step therapy has been completed

No invented drugs or impossible courses.

eight supervised physical therapy sessions over six weeks and a trial of naproxen

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: Denial claims step therapy not completed, but the patient completed PT, an NSAID trial, and normal radiographs — directly contradicting the factual basis; the patient clearly meets standard of care (positive McMurray, mechanical locking, effusion). Additionally the P2P window was verbal-only and never confirmed in writing. Strong clinical/factual and procedural vectors; easy appeal.
exploitable_weaknesses: Denial claims step therapy not completed, but the patient completed PT, NSAID trial, and normal radiographs
Peer-to-peer window conveyed only verbally and never confirmed in writing, an unverifiable procedural defect
strong_defenses: A legitimate step-therapy/conservative-management criterion exists and is correctly named (Cigna policy + MCG)
